title: Excess Capacity, Marginalq, and Corporate Investment
abstract: Theory posits that when managers anticipate excess capacity, averageqbecomes a biased estimator of marginalqas the potential for underutilizing new capital reduces the marginal benefit of investing. After correcting for this source of measurement error, the explanatory power of Tobin'sqsubstantially improves in time-series and cross-sectional regressions as well as in out-of-sample tests. These findings, together with a secular erosion in capacity utilization, help explain why corporate investment rates have been declining for decades despite averageqincreasing significantly. Our analysis indicates that economic rigidities have contributed to the persistent erosion in capacity utilization.

article.introduction:
Tobin'sqis often characterizedas the most widely accepted determinant of corporate investment (Erickson and Whited (2006)). The theory is straightforward: firms should invest more aggressively when the market value of the firm is high relative to its replacement value (Tobin (1969) and Hayashi (1982)). The logic underlying Tobin'sqis economically compelling, and hence the theory has spawned a large literature.
Casual observation of the data, however, is seemingly at odds with the model's central prediction. Although averageqhas been generally increasing over time, the rate of aggregate corporate investment spending has been declining (e.g., Jones and Philippon (2016), Alexander and Eberly (2018), and Fu, Huang, and Wang (2022)). Further complicating this puzzle is the fact that at the same time, corporate payouts have been increasing (Kahle and Stulz (2021)), implying that the collective decision of managers to slow down investment is voluntary and not driven by financial constraints.
One possible explanation for this puzzling empirical finding is that averageqis a poor proxy for the incremental value of an additional unit of productive capital (or marginalq), leading to a disconnect between averageqand investment. Although Hayashi (1982) shows that it is possible for averageqto equal marginalq, this theoretical result holds only under certain restrictive conditions. One such key condition is that the firm is always operating at full capacity. This assumption is critical because if managers face the prospect of operating below full capacity, theory then predicts that averageqwill be a biased estimator of marginalq, and hence the empirical relation between averageqand investment will break down.
To illustrate this point, consider today's economic climate in which many shopping malls stand empty as consumers shift to online shopping. Understandably, the value of the companies holding these vacant properties has declined significantly.1Now suppose this trend were to reverse and demand for mall space started to increase. Under this scenario the value of these properties would increase, as investors anticipate improved cash flow projections, which would cause averageqto increase. However, new investment would be limited until these companies exhaust their existing idle capacity. Thus, under this scenario, an empiricist would observe that investment rates decouple from a rising averageq.
This economic argument is formalized by Precious (1985) and Licandro (1992a,1992b), who develop theoretical models showing that as expected excess capacity increases, marginalqdiverges from averageq, making averageqan increasingly poor proxy for marginalq. This insight is significant, as most firms rarely operate at full capacity. According to survey data gathered by the Federal Reserve, average capacity utilization for industrial firms has been generally declining since the mid-1970s and has rarely exceeded 80% since 2000 (Pierce and Wisniewski (2018)). Given this stylized fact, it is possible that traditional investment regressions that do not control for expected excess capacity are misspecified.
In this paper, we account for this potential measurement error in averageqby including the ratio of sales to total capital (or asset utilization) in investment regressions. Asset utilization is a natural proxy for capacity utilization since firms experiencing excess capacity should display lower output-to-capital ratios relative to similar firms operating at full capacity. Consistent with this argument, we find that this variable is positively correlated with survey-based measures of capacity utilization from the Federal Reserve. Furthermore, we find that current levels of asset utilization are reliable predictors of future capacity utilization. This is an important finding because theory predicts that the wedge between average and marginalqshould be a function of managers’ expectations of future excess capacity.
We begin our analysis by estimating the parameters of the standardqmodel. Consistent with previous studies, we too confirm the paradoxical relation between aggregate corporate investment rates and aggregate averageqin time-series regressions. However, when we augment theqmodel by including asset utilization as a control variable to isolate the effect of expected excess capacity, the explanatory power of investment regressions increases substantially and the coefficient on averageqis generally positive. Furthermore, supporting the predictions in Precious (1985) and Licandro (1992a,1992b), we find that investment is positively correlated with the level of asset utilization (or negatively correlated with excess capacity). This is consistent with the idea that firms that expect future excess capacity have, on average, less need for new capital investment.2
These results are robust across a range of time periods, subsamples, and industries. Although Tobin's model is predicated on levels, controlling for expected excess capacity also significantly improves the explanatory power of regressions using first differences. This improvement extends to investment regressions using firm-level data. More importantly, we find that the augmented model significantly outperforms the traditionalqmodel in out-of-sample tests. Additional analysis further indicates that the key role of asset utilization in explaining investment is not subsumed by industry concentration levels.
One may argue that the additional explanatory power from the augmentedqmodel comes from controlling for financial constraints, as firms exhibiting elevated levels of asset utilization also tend to generate greater cash flows. Contrary to this prediction, however, our findings reveal that the improvement in explanatory power coming from the augmented model is greatest among the largest firms in our sample. This result is at odds with the implications of the financial constraints hypothesis, as prior evidence documents that financial frictions decline with firm size (e.g., Gertler and Gilchrist (1994), Perez-Quiros and Timmermann (2000), and Hadlock and Pierce (2010)). Corroborating these findings, we show that while large firms have better access to internally generated funds and to cheaper sources of external capital such as investment-grade debt and commercial paper, small firms rely more heavily on external equity to finance their operations. Given that equity financing is costlier than internal financing due to adverse selection and flotation costs (e.g., Myers and Majluf (1984) and Lee et al. (1996)), theory predicts that equity-dependent firms should be more sensitive to internally generated cash flows, as they can substitute expensive external capital for cheaper internal capital (Hennessy, Levy, and Whited (2007)).3This is the opposite of what we find. Our findings are more in line with the predictions of the excess capacity hypothesis since larger firms are more likely to have converted their growth options into productive assets (e.g., Berk, Green, and Naik (1999) and Trigeorgis and Lambertides (2014)), which would increase their likelihood of experiencing idle capacity when demand falls. In additional tests, we confirm that asset utilization proxies for capacity utilization rather than for financial constraints.
After obtaining a more substantive understanding of the role of expected excess capacity on investment policy, the next key question is why the rate of investment spending has declined so substantially. The answer lies in the unexpected finding that asset utilization has been persistently declining since the 1980s. After peaking at 1.73 in 1979, aggregate asset utilization fell by roughly half to 0.86 in 2021. Using cross-sectional analysis at the industry level, we show that the decline in investment rates is linked to the decline in asset utilization (or increase in excess capacity). Overall, these findings suggest that despite averageqrising over time, investment spending has been generally declining due to a buildup of excess capacity.
Surprisingly, this important trend in asset utilization does not show up in government data. Specifically, the ratio of value-added to nonfinancial assets, as reported by the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA), does not display a declining trend. We reconcile this discrepancy by showing that the BEA has been substantially underestimating the amount of intangible capital in the economy, leading to upward bias in their measure of asset utilization. While numerous studies document the increasing importance of intangible capital over the past several decades (e.g., Corrado, Hulten, and Sichel (2005)), the BEA's measures of intangible capital do not match this trend.4We show that this issue biases the trend in asset utilization significantly upward. This is not surprising given that in the past, the government has been slow to revise its data definitions to incorporate key structural changes in the economy (e.g., Corrado, Hulten, and Sichel (2005), Corrado and Hulten (2010), and Koh, Santaeulalia-Liopis, and Zheng (2020)).
By documenting that asset utilization has been declining over time, our paper offers a potential explanation for the negative relation between averageqand investment spending. This finding logically raises yet another important question: Why is the decline in asset utilization so persistent over time? We rule out several possibilities, including the impact of goodwill on the book value of assets after mergers and acquisitions (M&As), substitution of capital for labor (i.e., automation), and production inefficiencies. Instead, our results are more consistent with recent theoretical studies arguing that rigidities in labor and product markets limit the ability of managers to fully adjust production costs and prices during negative economic shocks, subsequently generating a persistent gap between product demand and potential output (e.g., Eggertsson and Krugman (2012), Michaillat and Saez (2015), and Rendahl (2016)). We provide evidence consistent with these rigidities, particularly around recessions.
Interestingly, our findings are not unique to the U.S. economy. Controlling for expected excess capacity is also important in explaining the decline in investment rates in other G7 countries. Taken together, our empirical evidence indicates that augmenting the traditionalqmodel to account for expected excess capacity is critical in mitigating the measurement error in averageq, as previously identified by Precious (1985) and Licandro (1992a,1992b).
Our paper makes several important contributions to the corporate investment literature. First, a large extant literature provides numerous explanations for why averageqmight be a weak predictor of investment. These include, among others, (i) measurement error (e.g., Philippon (2009) and Erickson and Whited (2012)), (ii) intangible assets (e.g., Peters and Taylor (2017) and Crouzet and Eberly (2023)), (iii) financial frictions (e.g., Gomes (2001) and Hennessy, Levy, and Whited (2007)), and (iv) decreasing competition (Gutiérrez and Philippon (2017)). Our paper investigates another key source of measurement error or bias in estimating marginalq, namely, the presence of expected excess capacity. When we account for this effect, the paradoxical findings betweenqand investment are no longer present. Instead, we find (i) a positive relation between investment and averageqin most time-series regressions and (ii) significant improvement in the explanatory power of Tobin'sqmodel in both in-sample and out-of-sample tests.
Second, contrary to recent studies that find evidence of a structural break in theq-model in the mid-1990s (e.g., Andrei, Mann, and Moyen (2019), and Lee, Shin, and Stulz (2021)), we find no such break after correcting for expected excess capacity. In general, Tobin's theory of marginalqhas been working well all along; however, the measurement error caused by not controlling for expected excess capacity has obscured the empirical relation between investment and averageq.
Third, we provide an economically plausible explanation for why corporate investment rates have been cut in half over the last four decades despite the substantial increase in averageqover time. Specifically, increasing excess capacity in the economy over the last several decades has reduced the marginal benefit of an extra unit of capital. This phenomenon seems to explain the persistent decline in investment rates documented in recent papers (e.g., Jones and Philippon (2016) and Alexander and Eberly (2018)). If firms can accommodate increased demand with existing spare capacity, it follows that managers will not necessarily increase investment spending despite rising levels of averageq.
Fourth, we provide evidence that rigidities in product and labor markets appear to be inhibiting the ability of managers to respond to contractions in demand, which provides a potential rational explanation for the persistence of excess capacity over time. This finding is consistent with predictions of recent theoretical models (e.g., Eggertsson and Krugman (2012), Michaillat and Saez (2015), Rendahl (2016), and Murphy (2017)).
Fifth, a large literature documents that contemporaneous cash flows are an important determinant of investment spending (e.g., Blanchard, Rhee, and Summers (1993) and Abel and Eberly (2011)). This result has been traditionally interpreted as evidence that financial constraints limit corporate investment (e.g., Fazzari, Hubbard, and Petersen (1988), Hoshi, Kashyap, and Scharfstein (1991), and Almeida and Campello (2007)). Our findings, however, diverge from this interpretation. Instead, we show that the positive cash flow-investment relation is more consistent with the excess capacity hypothesis than with the financial constraints hypothesis. These results confirm the conclusions reached in several studies that show that the impact of cash flows on investment is unrelated to financial frictions (e.g., Kaplan and Zingales (1997), Erickson and Whited (2000), Hadlock and Pierce (2010), Chen and Chen (2012), and Grullon, Hund, and Weston (2018)).
Finally, our paper highlights the importance of incorporating intangible assets into measures of productive capital when analyzing investment behavior. We show that ignoring intangible capital substantially overestimates measures of averageqand asset utilization, leading to potentially incorrect inferences in empirical studies. Researchers should be aware that this problem is quite severe when using aggregate data from the BEA.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the data and defines key variables. SectionIIreviews the paradoxical relation between investment and averageq. In SectionIII, we discuss and test the theoretical implications of incorporating expected excess capacity into Tobin's originalqmodel. We conduct a series of robustness checks in SectionIV. In SectionV, we link the secular decline in corporate investment to an increase in excess capacity. SectionVItests potential explanations for the increase in excess capacity over time, and SectionVIIexamines the international evidence. Finally, in SectionVIIIwe conclude.
article.1 Data and Variable Definitions:
Our primary sample consists of all firms in the Compustat data set in the period 1974 to 2021. We follow convention and exclude utilities (SIC codes 4900 to 4999), financial firms (6000 to 6999), and firms classified as public administration (9000 to 9999). We also exclude small firms with less than US$5 million in net property, plant, and equipment (Compustat itemppent) or sales (sale).
The variableIdenotes capital expenditures (capx). The variableSG&Ais equal to selling, general, and administrative expenses (xsga) minus research and development (R&D) expenses (xrd) minus in-processR&D(rdip); however, ifxrdis greater thanxsgabut less than the cost of goods sold (cogs), thenSG&Ais equal toxsga. BothR&DandSG&Aare set to zero if their values are missing. Following Hulten and Hao (2008), Eisfeldt and Papanikolaou (2014), and Peters and Taylor (2017), we assume that 30% ofSG&Ais investment in intangible capital.5The variableKdenotes net property, plant, and equipment, andKINTis the replacement cost of intangible capital calculated using the algorithm developed in Peters and Taylor (2017).6
The importance of adjusting for intangible assets is readily apparent in Panel A of Figure1. Using the definition of intangibles from Peters and Taylor (2017), this figure plots aggregate intangibles as a fraction of total capital (tangible plus intangible capital) over the period 1974 to 2021. Clearly, intangible assets constitute an increasingly important portion of the asset base of public U.S. firms over time. This dramatic increase in the prevalence of intangibles is consistent with the trend in U.S. patents granted over the same period, as can be seen in Panel B.7Thus, not adjusting for intangibles has the potential to dramatically affect economic measures such as investment, averageq, and capital productivity, measures that are critical in any study examining corporate investment policy.8
Figure2illustrates the bias that can arise when measuring averageqwithout incorporating intangible assets. We begin our analysis by sorting sample firms each year into deciles based on the percentage that intangibles constitute of their total capital (tangible plus intangible capital) at timet– 1. We then plot the empirical distribution of unadjusted and adjusted averageqratios for firms in the bottom (Panel A) and top (Panel B) deciles. Not surprisingly, Panel A shows that among low-intangibility firms, there is minimal disparity in the two distributions regardless of whether we account for intangibles. In contrast, Panel B shows that failing to adjust for intangibles among high-intangibility firms noticeably distorts the distribution of averageq. Here, the mean of the unadjustedqis approximately 15 times larger than that of the adjustedq(note that due to winsorization, there is a spike in the right tail of the distribution). The extremely large values that we observe for the unadjustedqrelative to the adjustedqare not surprising, as the unadjusted measure implicitly assumes that tangible capital is the only source of value for the firm. This evidence reinforces the argument in Peters and Taylor (2017) that excluding intangible capital from measures ofqand investment can bias empirical results, especially for firms with high levels of intangibility.9
article.2 The Investment-qParadox:
In this section, we analyze the time-series behavior of aggregate investment and averageq. In Figure3, we plot aggregate investment rates (solid line) and aggregate averageq(dashed line) over time. Consistent with evidence reported in previous papers (Jones and Philippon (2016), Gutiérrez and Philippon (2017), and Fu, Huang, and Wang (2022)), we see a downward trend in the rate of investment spending, starting from 24% in 1974 to 12% in 2021. Interestingly, the investment rate in 2021 is even lower than the investment rates that we observed during the worst part of the Great Recession in 2008. Under the assumption that averageqis an unbiased estimator of marginalq, neoclassical economics posits that if corporate investment rates are declining, then averageqshould display the same downward trend. Yet we see the opposite in Figure3, where averageqtriples from 0.7 in 1974 to 2.2 in 2021.
The trends discussed above relate to aggregate data. Figure4examines whether this phenomenon holds across industries. Here, we use Fama and French (1997) industry classification codes to sort firms by industry sector and report changes in averageqand changes in average investment spending rates between the beginning (1974 to 1976) and end (2019 to 2021) of our sample period. We see thatqtotincreases across all industry sectors while investment (itot) shows meaningful contractions across each industry sector. Clearly, the paradoxical relation between averageqand investment is not driven by a particular sector of the economy.
TableIformalizes the discussion above by reporting regression results from the traditionalqmodel in which the log of aggregate investment intensity (itot) is regressed on the log of lagged averageq(qtot).10Taking logs allows us to interpret the slope coefficients as elasticities and mitigates the effect of skewness in the variables. Contrary to the idea that averageqand investment rates should be positively correlated, Panel A of TableIshows that the coefficient on averageqis not only negative (−$ - $0.35) but also statistically significant at traditional confidence levels (t=−$\ - $2.82).
Recently, Andrei, Mann, and Moyen (2019) report an improvement in the traditionalqmodel after the mid-1990s, arguing that there was a structural break in the model around that time. We confirm this empirical result in our data. For the pre-1996 period (including 1996), we see that averageqand investment are negatively correlated whereas the relation is positive in the post-1996 period. Below, we investigate whether this structural break is robust to alternative specifications.
Finally, Panel B shows that the relation between the rate of investment and averageqacross industries is mostly negative. Only two industries display a positive coefficient on averageq(Energy and Telecommunications), but the coefficients are statistically insignificant at conventional levels. Overall, our findings indicate that the time-series behavior of aggregate investment rates cannot be explained by the traditionalqmodel.
article.3 Augmenting Tobin'sqModel to Control fo:
Conventional empirical tests of Tobin's theory are predicated on the assumption that averageqis an unbiased proxy for marginalq. However, this result breaks down if managers anticipate excess capacity in the future. Following the intuition in Precious (1985) and the methodological approach outlined in Obstfeld (1998), below we provide an analytical framework to assess the divergence between average and marginalqwhen demand is constrained and firms face the prospect of operating below full capacity.
Using the results above, we now examine the impact of constrained demand on the wedge between marginal and averageq. Hayashi (1982) shows that under certain conditions, there is no wedge as averageqequals marginalq, an assumption that many researchers have relied on in empirical examinations of Tobin's theory. Below, we show how demand constraints affect this important theoretical assumption.
Equation (23) shows that when the firm does not face demand constraints in the future (λ1(s) is equal to zero for alls), marginalqequals averageqas in Hayashi (1982). However, note that asλ1increases (i.e., as the firm becomes more demand-constrained), the wedge between marginal and averageqincreases. Licandro (1992b) finds a similar result in a setting in which the demand for the firm's products is stochastic.
In the model derived above, marginalqincreases as capacity utilization increases. In a related paper, Abel (1981) finds the opposite effect—as firms approach full capacity, they substitute labor for capital investment. However, his model examines a slightly different problem; in particular, he assumes that firms choose the level of capacity utilization they desireaftermaking an investment decision. Nonetheless, consistent with the theoretical implications developed in this paper, Abel (1981) also shows that when firms experience an exogenous demand shock, capital utilization and investment are positively related.
As discussed in the previous section, averageqis not a good proxy for marginalqin the presence of expected excess capacity. This may potentially cause problems for the empiricist who cannot observe marginalqand instead relies on averageqas a proxy for what is otherwise unobservable. To address this issue, we augment Tobin's traditionalqmodel by adding a proxy for expected excess capacity to mitigate the measurement error in averageqthat arises when managers expect to operate below full capacity. This approach allows us to control for the variation in marginalqthat comes from variation in expected excess capacity, thereby mitigating the bias (wedge) discussed above.
In this paper, we use asset utilization (i.e., the sales-to-total-capital ratio) as a proxy for expected excess capacity. The rationale is straightforward. Holding all else constant, as firms approach full capacity, they will mechanically have higher sales-to-total-capital ratios than comparable firms operating below full capacity. Below, we perform several empirical tests showing that asset utilization is a robust proxy for expected capacity utilization.
Using the standard approach in recent literature (e.g., Piketty and Zucman (2014)), we include intangible capital in measures of output relative to capital. This is an important adjustment because intangibles can have a significant effect on the productive capacity of the firm. In the literature, intangible capital has been treated as an additional factor of production that affects the capacity of a firm to produce products and services. For example, in a Cobb-Douglas framework, intangible capital interacts with labor and physical capital to generate output (e.g., Griliches (1979)). The idea here is that intangible capital, such as technological or organizational capital, significantly enhances the productivity of both labor and physical capital, thereby increasing the firm's potential output or total capacity. In this framework, without intangibles, the firm's potential capacity is severely constrained. For example, consider a firm like Google, which possesses a large amount of intangible capital (e.g., internally developed algorithms, logistical networks, operating models, employer-provided training, and R&D capabilities). When combined with its physical capital and labor, this intangible capital enhances Google's productive capacity; absent intangibles, the ability of Google's labor and physical assets to generate output would be severely impaired.
It is also important to note that the presence of intangible assets exposes the firm to greater risk of excess capacity. This is because intangibles constitute a more firm-specific form of investment compared to physical capital (e.g., Alderson and Betker (1996) and Hall and Lerner (2010)), making them less adaptable to redeployment. Hence, investing in intangibles entails a higher degree of irreversibility compared to physical capital.12As such, companies heavily dependent on intangible assets have less ability to adjust their capital during periods of reduced demand, thus increasing their exposure to operating with excess capacity in the future.
Given the discussion above, it is clear that including intangibles is important when calculating a firm's productive capital and potential excess capacity. To illustrate the bias that can arise when we exclude intangibles, in Figure5we plot the empirical distribution of sales-to-asset ratios adjusted and unadjusted for intangibles. Panels A and B report these distributions for the bottom and top deciles of intangibility, respectively. Panel A shows that among firms with low levels of intangibility, the distributions of the unadjusted and adjusted asset utilization measures are very similar. In contrast, Panel B shows that for firms ranked in the highest decile of intangibility, the average unadjusted ratio (15.8) is 11 times larger than the average adjusted ratio. Thus, ignoring intangibles can significantly inflate the productivity of assets in high-intangibility firms. Accordingly, we use measures of asset utilization that include intangible capital to reduce this potential bias. We find that the average adjusted measures of asset utilization for both high- and low-intangibility firms are not that different (1.09 vs. 1.40), a result one would expect in a competitive market where the free flow of capital should arbitrage away significant differences in the productivity of capital.
The model derived in this paper links marginalqto managers’ expectations of future asset utilization (see equation (12)). However, obtaining direct estimates of managers’ expectations across our entire sample is challenging. To address this issue, we examine whether current levels of asset utilization are a good proxy for future realizations of this measure.
We measure expected asset utilization using three approaches. First, we use sales at timet– 1 scaled by total capital at timet– 1 (CurrAU), a statistic available to managers as they prepare for investment in yeart. Second, because sales may change significantly in future years due to innovations managers are aware of at timet– 1, we use realized sales at timetscaled by total capital available at timet– 1 as a measure of expected asset utilization (ExpAU). This measure assumes that the manager can reliably forecast realized asset utilization for yeartwith information at their disposal at the end of yeart– 1.13Finally, we use an average of these two measures as a third estimate of expected asset utilization (AvgAU). An advantage of using this third proxy is that it smooths out any temporary shock inCurrAUorExpAU.
To investigate whether these proxies are reliable measures of managers’ long-term expectations of future asset utilization, we estimate Fama and MacBeth (1973) regressions relating future levels of asset utilization to our proxies for expected asset utilization. In the first stage, we estimate cross-sectional coefficients each year using all observations in that year. In the second stage, we compute time-series means of the cross-sectional regression coefficients. We use this approach to avoid potential look-ahead bias that may arise in panel data regressions. We also include as a control the log value of total capital at timet– 1 in all specifications. To mitigate the effect of outliers, we winsorize all ratios at the 1stand 99thpercentiles of their distributions.
The results, reported in TableII, reveal that our proxies for asset utilization reliably predict future levels of asset utilization over different horizons. For the one-year-ahead forecast, the average elasticity of asset utilization at timet+ 1 relative to our proxies is approximately 0.9 with an averageR2of 83%. Even though the magnitude of the coefficient declines as we consider longer horizons, the average elasticity of asset utilization at timet+ 5 relative to our proxies is approximately 0.8 with anR2of about 68%. This evidence indicates that, on average, current levels of asset utilization are reasonable proxies for what managers may be expecting in the future.
In this subsection, we examine whether asset utilization is correlated with direct measures of capacity utilization. We gather capacity utilization measures at the industry level from the Federal Reserve Economic Data website.14These measures are derived from the U.S Census Bureau's quarterly survey, in which managers provide information on (i) the market value of actual production and (ii) the market value of production as if the plant had been operating at full capacity.15The survey responses come from approximately 7,500 plant managers in charge of operations with five or more production workers.16These surveys also record the reasons managers provide as to why they are operating below full capacity. A limitation of this data set is that it is only available at the industry level for utilities, manufacturing, and mining firms. Nevertheless, this information is valuable as it allows us to examine the extent to which asset utilization is directly tied to managers’ assessments of excess capacity.
Figure6plots the average level of capacity utilization as reported in these surveys sorted by asset utilization decile. To perform this analysis, we calculate aggregate measures of asset utilization using Compustat data at the industry level and match the resulting data set with the corresponding capacity utilization data for industries reported by the Federal Reserve. We control for both time-invariant heterogeneity across industries and aggregate systematic shocks by estimating decile averages using panel data regressions that include both industry and year fixed effects. Looking across deciles, we see a positive, near-monotonic relation between asset utilization and capacity utilization as reported by the Federal Reserve. Moreover, the difference in capacity utilization between the lowest and highest deciles is both statistically and economically significant. Moving from the lowest to the highest decile increases capacity utilization by 950 basis points (bps). To put this change in perspective, the mean shock to aggregate capacity utilization around recessions has been approximately 890 bps.
In sum, our analysis indicates that current asset utilization, which is observable for all firms, appears to be a reasonable proxy for expected capacity utilization. Accordingly, in the next section we use this variable to control for the potential effect of expected excess capacity on investment.
The theoretical arguments developed in Precious (1985) and Licandro (1992a,1992b) lead to two key empirical predictions. Specifically, if expected excess capacity affects the marginal benefit of investment, then controlling for expected excess capacity should (i) reduce the wedge between marginal and averageq, thus making averageqa better proxy for marginalq, and (ii) significantly improve the explanatory power of investment regressions.
To test these predictions, in TableIIIwe augment Tobin'sqmodel with asset utilization to mitigate potential measurement error in averageq. The results are quite compelling. In Panel A, using the full-sample period, the regression coefficient onqtotflips from negative to positive. This result is consistent with the key idea that averageqbecomes a better proxy for marginalqwhen controlling for expected excess capacity. Furthermore, the coefficients on the different proxies for expected capacity utilization are positive, indicating that investment is an increasing function of capacity utilization (or stated differently, a decreasing function of excess capacity). Note that including asset utilization as a regressor significantly improves the explanatory power of Tobin's model.
As mentioned earlier, Andrei, Mann, and Moyen (2019) find that the relation between investment and averageqimproves after 1996, suggesting a structural break in Tobin'sqmodel. In Panels B and C, we investigate whether this structural break exists after controlling for expected excess capacity. We find no evidence of such a break—the augmented model works equally well in both the pre- and post-1996 time periods. The regression coefficient on averageqis positive and statistically significant in both subsamples. Overall, this evidence supports Tobin'sqtheory of investment. Marginalqappears to have always worked, but measurement error due to excess capacity appears to have obscured the relation.
Next, we verify the robustness of the augmented model and investigate whether our general findings are perhaps driven by a particular industry sector. TableIVreports regression coefficients for the augmentedqmodel by industry. In contrast to the results from the traditional model, the augmentedqmodel generally works well across each industry. Furthermore, it shows remarkable power in explaining investment independent of which proxy we use for expected asset utilization. In Panel A, where we only use information available at the end of yeart– 1 to define asset utilization, the regression coefficient forqflips from mostly negative to mostly positive, with five of the 10 industries showing significance at traditional confidence levels. Without controlling for asset utilization, the coefficient on averageqis negative and significant in six of the 10 industries (see Panel B of TableI). Panels B and C report similar findings usingExpAUand the average ofExpAUandCurrAUas proxies for expected excess capacity. In each of these panels, we see that controlling for asset utilization significantly increases the explanatory power of the regressions relative to theR2’s reported for the traditionalqmodel in Panel B of TableI. TableIValso shows that asset utilization is an important determinant of investment in each industry, as all 10 industries show a positive relation between asset utilization and investment.
article.4 Robustness:
For completeness, we now examine the robustness of the augmentedqmodel.
Corporate investment programs often span several years, as do trends in relative market valuation that can affect averageq. As such, one might be concerned that by using levels of investment andqto estimate Tobin's model,t-statistics may be inflated due to potential serial correlation. In previous tables, we mitigate this concern by adjusting allt-statistics using Newey and West (1987) standard errors. However, as an additional check, we estimate investment regressions using first differences to further address the potential effect of persistent regressors (Barro (1990)).
TableVreports coefficient estimates for both the traditional and augmentedqmodels, where we regress changes in aggregate investment rates on lagged changes inq. Focusing first on the traditionalqmodel (the first column in each of the panels) for the overall time period (Panel A) and for each of the two subperiods (Panels B and C), the point estimate on the change inqtotis positive but either insignificant or only marginally significant at traditional confidence levels. Moreover, theR2’s are modest, ranging from 0.02 for the pre-1996 subperiod to 0.18 for the post-1996 subperiod.
In contrast, columns (2) through (4) show that when we augment investment regressions by including proxies for expected excess capacity, the explanatory power of the models improves significantly. One caveat, however, is that these results are predicated on changes that do not capture the effect of relative levels as implied in Tobin'sqmodel. To some degree, this alters the economic interpretation of the coefficients. Nevertheless, even using first differences, the augmented model outperforms the traditional model.
To further check the robustness of the augmentedqmodel, we consider evidence using firm-level data. Although researchers have struggled to find evidence supporting the traditionalqmodel using aggregate data, the extant literature does find some support using firm-level data. The main reason for this discrepancy is that aggregate investment is driven primarily by large firms, whose investment-qsensitivity is negative and differs remarkedly from that of smaller firms (e.g., Grullon, Hund, and Weston (2018)).
An advantage of using firm-level data is that one can account for unobserved time-invariant heterogeneity across firms. A disadvantage, however, is that firm-level data are noisy, especially among smaller firms, which can hinder our ability to obtain clear inferences. Another drawback is that this approach weighs each firm in our sample equally regardless of size, which limits our ability to make inferences about the broader economy. Nevertheless, we use this approach to continue investigating the robustness of the augmented model ofqand its ability to explain investment spending.
TableVIreports coefficients for both the traditional and augmentedqmodels using firm-level data and controlling for firm and year fixed effects. To mitigate the effect of outliers, we winsorize all variables at the 1stand 99thpercentiles. Consistent with prior literature, the coefficients forqtotin the traditional model are positive (see column (1) in Panels A through C) and show similar elasticities over the two subperiods. However, after we control for asset utilization, theR2’s of the investment regressions significantly increase across all time periods. Thus, consistent with earlier findings, asset utilization remains an important determinant of investment.
In summary, the augmented model significantly improves the explanatory power of Tobin'sqmodel using panel data.
One alternative explanation for our results is that asset utilization is proxying for financial constraints. This is a reasonable argument, as cash flows are an increasing function of the productivity of capital. However, at least at the aggregate level, we know that the decline in investment rates is unlikely to be related to financial constraints, as aggregate net payouts (dividends plus share repurchases minus equity issues scaled by total capital) have been increasing over time (see Panel A of FigureIA.1in theInternet Appendix). Moreover, the persistent decline in investment rates has occurred during a period in which U.S. long-term interest rates have fallen from approximately 7.4% in 1974 to 1.5% in 2021 (see Panel B of FigureIA.1).
However, to formally disentangle the financial constraints story from the excess capacity hypothesis, we examine the effect of total productive capital on the explanatory power of the augmented model. As firms increase their scale, managers convert their growth options into productive capital (e.g., Berk, Green, and Naik (1999)). Consequently, if investment is irreversible, larger firms are more susceptible to experiencing excess capacity during periods of negative demand shocks compared to their smaller counterparts, as larger firms possess relatively higher levels of productive capital in place (e.g., Trigeorgis and Lambertides (2014)). This implies that if asset utilization is mitigating measurement error in averageqdue to expected excess capacity, then the improvement from using the augmentedqmodel should be notably more pronounced for larger firms. In contrast, if the measurement error in averageqis coming from financial constraints, then the augmentedqmodel should be more relevant for small firms that tend to be more vulnerable to financial frictions (e.g., Gertler and Gilchrist (1994), Perez-Quiros and Timmermann (2000), and Hadlock and Pierce (2010)).
To test these distinct predictions, TableVIIreports regression coefficients for both the traditional and augmentedqmodels across deciles formed at the beginning of each year on the basis of total capital (tangible plus intangible capital). Panel A shows that when estimating the traditionalqmodel, the coefficients onqtotfor smaller-decile firms are positive and significant, consistent with the evidence in Baker, Stein, and Wurgler (2003). However, as we increase firm size, the coefficient onqtotdecreases and ultimately becomes negative. As such, the empirical evidence indicates that the failure of the traditionalqmodel is driven mainly by large firms.
When we augment theqmodel and include asset utilization in investment regressions (Panels B, C, and D), the coefficients onqtotfor large firms are no longer negative—the coefficients are positive and statistically significant across all size deciles. More importantly, the regression coefficients onqtotfor the largest firms in our sample experience the greatest increase when we control for asset utilization. As we look across size deciles, the explanatory power of the augmentedqmodel is high across each decile relative to the traditional model, yet the greatest improvement is observed in larger firms. This evidence is consistent with the predictions of the excess capacity hypothesis.
The previous results can only be consistent with the financial constraint hypothesis if, on average, large firms are somehow more financially constrained than small firms. Although prior literature indicates that this is not the case (e.g., Gertler and Gilchrist (1994), Perez-Quiros and Timmermann (2000), and Hadlock and Pierce (2010)), we perform a series of tests to further rule out this possibility. Specifically, we examine how firm size is related to the following proxies for financial frictions: (i) the ability of firms to internally generate funds, (ii) firms’ access to public debt and commercial paper markets, and (iii) firms’ reliance on equity capital, one of the most expensive forms of external financing (Myers and Majluf (1984) and Lee et al. (1996)).
In Panels A and B of Figure7, we plot average net cash flows and net total payouts sorted by total capital, respectively.17Comparatively speaking, larger firms show few signs of being financially constrained. They generate more than sufficient internal funds to both finance their investment needs and return substantial levels of capital to shareholders. In contrast, smaller firms are less able to generate sufficient internal capital to cover their investment needs. Indeed, these firms have negative net payouts, indicating that they depend heavily on external equity financing.
Panels C, D, and E summarize the ability of firms to access debt markets by reporting the fraction of firms with rated debt, investment-grade debt (BBB- or above), and commercial paper ratings across size deciles, respectively.18Roughly 80% of the firms in Decile 10 possess rated debt, with 70% of these firms holding an investment-grade debt rating. In addition, more than half of the firms in Decile 10 have access to commercial paper. Conversely, all of these proportions are close to zero in Deciles 1 through 5, indicating that smaller firms typically do not have easy access to the debt market or the commercial paper market.
Overall, this evidence strongly indicates that larger firms face fewer financial frictions compared to smaller firms. As such, financial constraints are unlikely to explain our findings in TableVII. These results are consistent with those reported by Grullon, Hund, and Weston (2018), who also show that the sensitivity of investment to cash flows is increasing in size, a result that once again contradicts the predictions of the financial constraints hypothesis. Other studies also cast serious doubt on the idea that the positive correlation between cash flows and investment is related to financial frictions (e.g., Kaplan and Zingales (1997), Erickson and Whited (2000), Hadlock and Peirce ((2010)), and Chen and Chen (2012)).
We next perform one more test to distinguish the excess capacity hypothesis from the financial constraints argument by examining how short-term sales react to changes in investment opportunities. To motivate this test, consider the example we discussed earlier regarding empty shopping malls in the United States. If there is an increase in demand for shopping mall space, averageqwill rise as expected cash flows increase. Under this scenario, if asset utilization proxies for financial constraints, malls with higher asset utilization (and thus generating higher cash flows) will be better positioned to finance their short-term needs to meet the sudden increase in demand (Almeida, Carvalho, and Kim (2024)). Conversely, if asset utilization proxies for capacity utilization, malls with relatively high asset utilization will be capacity-constrained in the near term, which will inhibit their ability to lease space to keep up with the spike in demand. Thus, according to the financial constraints (excess capacity) hypothesis, the sensitivity of short-term sales growth to averageqshould increase (decrease) with asset utilization.
To test these predictions, we regress sales growth at timeton the log of averageqat timet– 1, the log of asset utilization at timet– 1, a term interacting the previous two variables, and the log of total capital at timet– 1. We use firm-level data for this analysis and include year and firm fixed effects. To mitigate the effect of outliers, all ratios are winsorized at the 1stand 99thpercentiles of their distributions. In this specification, the coefficient on the interaction term captures how the sensitivity of short-term sales growth to growth prospects varies with the level of asset utilization. The financial constraints hypothesis predicts that this coefficient should be positive while the excess capacity hypothesis predicts that it should be negative.
The results from this analysis are reported in TableVIII. Consistent with the idea that firms with better growth prospects tend to grow faster, the first column shows that the coefficient on averageqis positive and statistically significant. However, the coefficient on the interaction term is negative and significant, supporting the argument that firms with high levels of asset utilization face capacity constraints. Given that the relation between asset utilization and sales growth opportunities may be nonlinear (i.e., capacity constraints may be more binding for firms with extremely high asset utilization ratios), we replace the continuous asset utilization variable with asset utilization decile dummies. The results reported in the second column indicate that the sensitivity of short-term sales growth to growth prospects is weakest among firms with the highest levels of asset utilization (Decile 10), a result again consistent with the capacity utilization hypothesis.
Overall, the evidence in this subsection indicates that asset utilization is capturing capacity utilization rather than financial constraints.
Thus far our analysis has been based on in-sample regressions. While common in the literature, this approach has several limitations. For example, factors such as noisy coefficients and/or structural breaks in the time series can render strong in-sample models unreliable when making out-of-sample forecasts. We perform out-of-sample tests to address this potential issue. This analysis also addresses the concern that our in-sample results from the augmented model may be driven by spurious in-sample correlations.
Accordingly, we run 20-year rolling-window recursive regressions to forecast investment rates at timetusing only information available at timet−$\ - $1. We then compare the mean squared error (MSE) of the augmented model to that obtained from (i) the traditionalqmodel and (ii) a naïve model in which we simply use the average investment rate over the past 20 years as our forecast for the following year. As a further check, we also verify the robustness of this analysis by using an expanding-window instead of a rolling-window approach.
Panel A of Figure8plots annual forecasts of aggregate investment rates using a rolling-window approach from each of the investment models along with the actual investment rate. Consistent with the in-sample evidence, the augmentedqmodel significantly outperforms the two alternative forecasting models. The MSE of the augmented model is 0.0098%; this is 89% lower than the MSE of the traditionalqmodel. Interestingly, the traditionalqmodel does not perform much better than the naïve model (respective MSEs of 0.0881% and 0.0902%). Also note that although the traditionalqmodel forecasts a decline in investment over time, it does so for the wrong reasons. Because the regression coefficient onqis negative for most of the forecasting period, this model spuriously predicts lower investment rates asqhas been increasing over time. We obtain similar results when we use the expanding-window approach (see Panel B).
Overall, the augmented model significantly outperforms other investment models out-of-sample. The evidence also suggests our results are not driven by spurious in-sample correlations.
One might argue that asset utilization may be proxying for the evolution in product markets over time. With decreased competition and increased opportunity for monopolistic behavior, firms could simply be reducing investment spending to limit output, thereby extracting rents from consumers (e.g., Lindenberg and Ross (1981) and Gutiérrez and Philippon (2017)).
In TableIA.IIin theInternet Appendix, we explore this possibility by examining whether the inclusion of a sales-weighted measure of economy-wide industry concentration, as proxied by the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI), subsumes the effect of asset utilization.19We find no evidence that the relation between asset utilization and investment is affected by increasing industry concentration. However, given that we are collapsing cross-sectional information regarding industry concentration across many firms into a single aggregate measure at a given point in time, one might argue that our measure of concentration is too coarse. To address this potential issue, we estimate the same regression using panel-level data (see TableIA.III). The conclusion does not change—asset utilization is not subsumed by industry concentration. This is true for the full period as well as for the pre- and post-1996 subperiods.
Overall, asset utilization and its association with the rate of investment spending is robust to the inclusion of industry concentration levels.
Our analysis thus far uses the algorithm developed by Peters and Taylor (2017) to adjust measures of investment andqfor intangible assets. To check whether our results are sensitive to how we measure intangibles, we use an alternative approach recently developed by Ewens, Peters, and Wang (2025) to estimate intangibles.20One advantage of this approach is that it uses transaction prices from acquisitions and liquidations to calculate the market value of internally created intangible assets. Another advantage is that instead of assuming that 30% of SG&A is investment in intangible capital for all firms, this approach allows this proportion to vary across industries. Using this alternative measure of intangibles, we replicate our main analysis reported earlier in TableIII. For comparison purposes, we also include estimates from the traditionalqmodel. The results from this analysis validate our previous findings (see TableIA.IV).
article.5 Why Is Investment Declining Over Time?:
We conclude from the analyses above that augmenting the traditionalqmodel with asset utilization to control for expected excess capacity appears to be critical. Doing so substantially improves our ability to explain corporate investment. Nevertheless, a key question that remains is why the rate of corporate investment has been persistently declining over time despite the large increase in averageqevident over the same period.
A potential explanation is that excess capacity has been gradually accumulating in the economy over time. We explore this hypothesis in Panel A of Figure9by plotting the time series of the aggregate sales-to-total-capital ratio. We see a persistent decline in asset utilization starting around 1980.21After peaking at 1.73 in 1979, asset utilization fell by half to 0.86 by 2021. Given the strong sensitivity of investment to asset utilization, we can now appreciate the collective impact this factor has on investment. Although increases in averageqare associated with additional investment, these gains are more than offset by the negative influence of declining asset utilization. Consequently, the net effect is that the rate of corporate investment is declining over time.
To further validate this trend, in Panel B we check whether the same trend holds in survey-based capacity utilization data reported by the Federal Reserve. Here, individual managers report capacity utilization levels at their respective plants. Although the data are mainly limited to manufacturing firms, we see the same general downward trend.
To examine the robustness of the decline in asset utilization across the economy, we examine changes in mean asset utilization by industry sector. Figure10plots the change in asset utilization from the beginning of our sample period (1974 to 1976) to the end (2019 to 2021) by industry sector. Except for Telecommunications, all industries show a substantial contraction in asset utilization.
TableIXreports the results. When we only include changes inqtotas an explanatory variable (column (1)), we again find the puzzling result that industries that sustained the largest increase in averageqalso sustained the largest decline in investment rates. However, when we include changes in asset utilization as an additional control (columns (2) through (4)), the negative relation between changes inqtotand changes in investment becomes insignificant. We also find a strong positive correlation between changes in asset utilization and changes in investment rates in the cross section. The positive and statistically significant coefficient on the change in asset utilization indicates that industries experiencing the greatest decline in asset utilization over time also experience the greatest decline in investment.
As discussed earlier, excluding intangible assets in measures of capital productivity has the potential to overstate the productivity of capital, particularly in firms with high levels of intangibility. Given that intangible investment is becoming more prevalent in the economy over time, scaling sales only by tangible assets can generate a spurious upward trend in asset utilization.
In TableX, we explore the consequences of excluding intangibles on the trend in asset utilization by reporting estimated coefficients from time-series regressions relating asset utilization to a time trend variable across portfolios based on intangibility levels. Panel A shows that when we exclude intangible assets from the sales-to-asset ratio, the coefficients on the time trend variable for firms with low levels of intangibility are negative (see Deciles 1 through 6). Not surprisingly, however, the coefficients for firms with high levels of intangibility are positive and statistically significant at traditional confidence levels (see Deciles 7 through 10). Panel B shows that once we scale sales by total capital (tangible plus intangible capital), each of the 10 deciles displays a downward trend in asset utilization. Overall, ignoring intangibles, particularly among firms with high levels of intangibility, hides the negative trend otherwise observed at the aggregate level.
The preceding analysis also reveals that the decline in asset utilization is particularly pronounced among firms with high levels of intangible assets, which is consistent with the idea that intangible assets have a higher degree of irreversibility than physical capital as they constitute a more firm-specific form of investment (e.g., Alderson and Betker (1996) and Hall and Lerner (2010)).
Many researchers use aggregate data supplied by the U.S. government to measure the productivity of capital. For completeness, we use these data to create a measure similar to asset utilization for nonfinancial corporate businesses. Specifically, using data from Table S.5.a as reported by the BEA in the Integrated Macroeconomic Accounts, we scale gross value-added (Line 1) by nonfinancial assets (Line 109).23We report the time-series behavior of this ratio in Panel A of Figure11. Contrary to the declining trend documented using Compustat data, this proxy for asset utilization estimated using government data is relatively flat over time.
A critical reason for this discrepancy relates to how the government records intangible assets. When measuring intangibles, the government only captures software, R&D, and private entertainment originals. This measure closely resembles Peters and Taylor's (1997) measure of knowledge capital (a subset of all possible sources of intangible investment), which they define as the portion of intangible capital coming from R&D.24TableIA.VIshows that over the period 1974 to 2021, this definition of knowledge capital accounted for, on average, 91.5% of the BEA's measure of intangibles. It is important to note, however, that Peters and Taylor (1997) include not only knowledge capital but also two other meaningful sources of intangible assets: organizational capital (e.g., business processes and systems, firm-specific know-how) and the value of other intangibles reported on the balance sheet.25TableIA.VIshows that after including these other intangibles, the BEA measure of intangibles accounts for less than 28% of total intangible assets. As such, the BEA has been materially understating the true extent of intangibles in the economy. This evidence is in line with the findings in Corrado, Hulten, and Sichel (2005), who document that over the period 1988 to 2000, the intangibles reported in the National Income and Product Accounts (NIPA) accounts represent, on average, less than 20% of total intangibles—implying that 80% of intangibles are underreported by the BEA.
This incomplete approach for addressing intangibles is not surprising, as prior research concludes that the government has been slow to incorporate intangible assets into its measures of total capital (e.g., Corrado et al. (2022)). For example, the BEA started formally incorporating R&D into the NIPA data only in 2013 even though the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) has been requiring firms to report R&D expenses since 1975.
In Panel B of Figure11, we report the trends in both tangible and intangible capital as a percentage of total capital using data from Table S.5.a from the BEA.26Surprisingly, according to the BEA, intangible capital represents, on average, less than 8.6% of total capital over the period 1974 to 2021. This contrasts sharply with the average ratio of 47.7% one obtains using Compustat data and applying Peters and Taylor's (2017) approach to measuring intangibles. As discussed above, this result is not surprising given that the BEA measure of intangibles accounts for less than 28% of total intangibles. The levels of intangibility derived from aggregate BEA data also do not square up with the recent evidence in Belo et al. (2022). Based on estimates from a structural model, they find that, on average, physical capital accounts for 22% to 30% of firms’ market value, while intangibles such as knowledge and brand capital account for 43% to 56%, with labor accounting for the balance of firm value.
Perhaps even more surprising, the ratio of intangible to total capital constructed using government data shows little change over time. For example, over the period 1995 to 2021, this ratio increased only slightly from 9.5% to 11.3%. This contrasts with the dramatic increase in intangible capital over the past several decades that has been documented in several studies (e.g., Corrado, Hulten, and Sichel (2005), Peters and Taylor (2017), and Ewens, Peters, and Wang (2025)). Moreover, it is extremely puzzling that the government data suggest that real estate represents the largest component of productive capital in the economy, even in 2021 (see Panel C of Figure11). Inexplicably, according to the BEA data, even as recently as 2021, real estate represents 58% of productive capital while intangibles comprise only 11%. Collectively, these findings seem implausible given the well-documented trend that the United States has been moving toward a knowledge-based economy.
To thoroughly evaluate the economic repercussions of the BEA's underestimation of intangibles on asset utilization, we perform two counterfactual exercises. In the first exercise, we modify the level of intangibles reported by the BEA by adding to it Peters and Taylor's (2017) estimates of organizational capital and other intangibles reported on the balance sheet. In the second exercise, we alter the original level of intangible assets reported by the BEA such that the ratio of intangible to physical capital using their data matches that based on Peters and Taylor's (2017) more comprehensive method. We then take these two hypothetical time series of intangible assets and calculate two new hypothetical time series of asset utilization. Figure12plots asset utilization using actual BEA data against the two hypothetical measures after normalizing each time series by its respective level in 1974. Unlike the flat trend observed using actual BEA data, the new hypothetical asset utilization ratios experience a decline. In particular, when we use the hypothetical intangibles from the first (second) counterfactual exercise, asset utilization declines by nearly 30% (40%) over the period 1974 to 2021. The declines we observe in the counterfactual analysis resemble what we see using the more comprehensive approach with Compustat information, again leading one to conclude that the understatement in the BEA's measure of intangibles is economically significant.27
article.6 Why is Asset Utilization Declining?:
Our analysis up to this point indicates that including asset utilization in investment regressions mitigates a major source of measurement error in averageq. Furthermore, the evidence indicates that the decline in the rate of investment is related to a secular decline in asset utilization. Yet this finding only raises another important question: why is asset utilization declining? In this section, we investigate several potential explanations.
A growing literature posits that frictions in product and labor markets hamper the ability of managers to adjust their operations in response to negative demand shocks. For example, Michaillat (2012), Michaillat and Saez (2015), Eggertsson and Krugman (2012), and Rendahl (2016) all show how rigidities in product prices and wages can generate excess capacity by limiting the ability of managers to stimulate demand during recessions. A common theme in these explanations is that permanent shocks to the output gap (potential minus actual production) occur primarily during recessionary periods.
Panel A of Figure13tests this prediction by examining the impact of recessions on asset and capacity utilization. This figure plots the average annual change in asset utilization conditional on whether the NBER classified a given year as expansionary or recessionary. During years of economic expansion, asset utilization declines but only at an average rate of −0.2% per year. However, during recessionary years, the average annual decrease is roughly 27 times larger, at −5.4%.
To formally examine whether the persistent decline in asset utilization is driven by economic rigidities, we perform two tests. First, if rigidities are not binding, then we should observe some type of deflationary process around recessions as managers reduce prices to stimulate demand. In contrast, if rigidities limit managers’ ability to reduce product prices during negative economic shocks, then we should not observe deflation around these shocks. To investigate this issue, in Panel B of Figure13we examine capacity utilization and aggregate price indices during the 48 months surrounding NBER recessions. Here, we plot mean monthly capacity utilization levels obtained from the Federal Reserve around each recession between 1970 and 2020. We normalize these levels by the capacity utilization observed at the trough of each respective recession. To examine how inflation behaves around these recessionary windows, we also plot cumulative inflation measures (i.e., mean and median Consumer Price Index (CPI) and Producer Price Index (PPI)). In the 24 months preceding recessions, capacity utilization falls, on average, 15.5%. Coming out of recessions, capacity utilization initially rebounds but levels off after about a year at roughly 40% below its prerecession level. Consistent with the notion that prices are rigid, we see no evidence of deflation around these negative economic shocks. This result suggests that managers are hampered in their ability to adjust prices to stimulate demand during recessions.
A second test of the rigidity story examines the sensitivity of operating costs to sales. If cost rigidities are binding, we should observe a weak link between operating costs and sales. Therefore, the sensitivity of operating costs to sales should decrease as rigidities strengthen. In contrast, if economic rigidities are economically trivial, then we should observe a stronger link between operating costs and sales as managers have more flexibility to adjust production costs in response to changes in product demand.
TableXIreports estimated coefficients from panel data regressions relating the annual growth in operating costs (cost of goods sold plus SG&A and R&D) to the annual growth in sales for subperiods between 1974 and 2021. These regressions include firm and year fixed effects, and the variables have been winsorized at their 1stand 99thpercentiles. We see that the relation between operating costs and sales was relatively high in the late 1970s, with a coefficient of 0.92. However, this relation has weakened over time to the point that the coefficient over the period 2015 to 2021 is 0.53. Overall, these findings are consistent with the notion that cost rigidities are not only economically important but also increasingly binding over time.
As a robustness check, we investigate and rule out several other potential explanations as to why asset utilization is declining.
One argument for why asset utilization is declining relates to the potential impact of arbitrary accounting rules on reported assets after M&A transactions. If mergers result in the generation of goodwill, assets will naturally increase, as any premium paid over book value for the target will inflate the combined assets of the new surviving firm. This seems an unlikely explanation given that survey data from managers show that capacity utilization is also declining (see Panel B of Figure9)—managers have no reason to change their views regarding capacity utilization simply due to the presence of an accounting convention.
Nevertheless, we check for this possibility in FigureIA.2, where we plot asset utilization after removing the effect of goodwill (gdwl) from total capital. After this adjustment, the trend in asset utilization continues to be negative.
Another plausible reason for the decline in asset utilization could be the increasing role of automation in the economy. Such a shift would cause the ratio of sales to total capital to decline mechanically over time. For example, for a given level of sales, substituting capital for labor would cause assets on the balance sheet to rise, reducing asset utilization even if sales were unchanged. At first glance, the trend in corporate investment is inconsistent with this hypothesis. If increasing automation is materially displacing labor, we would expect to see a corresponding substantial increase in investment spending. This contrasts with the secular decline evident in the rate of investment, particularly in recent decades when the trend toward automation is most prevalent.
Nevertheless, if the decline in asset utilization documented in this paper is driven by the substitution of capital for labor, we should observe a significant decline in the contribution of labor to total output. To investigate this possibility, FigureIA.3plots aggregate labor share (i.e., the percentage of output accruing to workers in the form of compensation) as published by the Federal Reserve.28Although the labor share declines slightly from 62.9% at the beginning of our sample period (1974 to 1976) to 59.6% at the end (2017 to 2019), this change is too modest to explain the substantial decline evident in asset utilization. Even if we attribute the entire decline in the labor share to an increase in the capital share, this implies that the capital share rose from 37.1% at the beginning of our sample period (1974 to 1976) to 40.4% at the end (2017 to 2019). One caveat regarding this result is that it does not correct for the bias in the labor share identified by Koh, Santaeulalia-Liopis, and Zheng (2020). These authors find that after correcting for the accounting treatment of intangibles gradually implemented by the BEA, the negative trend in the labor share disappears. This result contradicts the idea that the decline in asset utilization is driven by the substitution of capital for labor.
It is important to note, however, that the discussion above relies on the strong assumption that the decline observed in the labor share was absorbed completely by an increase in the capital share. Recent evidence by Barkai (2020) challenges this view. He provides empirical evidence that both labor and capital shares have been declining over time and are being displaced by increasing markups. More importantly, he finds that the decline in the capital share is larger than the decline in the labor share. If true, this suggests that the decline in asset utilization documented in this paper cannot be satisfactorily explained by a substitution of capital for labor.
While difficult to believe given the remarkable technological advances witnessed in recent decades, another possible explanation for the decline in asset utilization may be that firms have become less efficient over time. On the surface, this story is inconsistent with the fact that these same firms had much higher production levels, on average, just before economic downturns. Nevertheless, if this alternative explanation were true, one would expect to see a downward trend in asset utilization stemming from supply-side constraints rather than demand-side factors.
To test this explanation, we return to the survey data on capacity utilization published by the Federal Reserve. Apart from assessing managers' reports on plant utilization levels, the Federal Reserve, starting in 1997, also reports the reasons managers cite for their plants not operating at full capacity in the Quarterly Survey of Plant Capacity Utilization. Consistent with the evidence in Stahl and Morin (2013), FigureIA.4shows that the decline in capacity utilization appears to be driven primarily by demand issues. Specifically, over the past several decades, approximately 75% of surveyed managers state “insufficient orders” as the main reason for excess capacity. Although “insufficient supply of local labor force/skills” and “insufficient material” experienced a spike during the 2020 pandemic, “insufficient orders” has been the predominant reason managers mention for low levels of capacity utilization over the entire time series. In sum, production inefficiencies do not appear to be a major issue.
article.7 International Evidence:
In this section, we examine whether our main findings hold in other countries using data from Worldscope. Following the same sample formation criteria as described in SectionII, we gather accounting data from other G7 countries.29To construct measures of intangible assets for international firms, we follow the algorithm in Peters and Taylor (2017) (see SectionIVof theInternet Appendixfor details). For consistency, we also regenerate aggregate variables for the United States using the Worldscope database.
We begin our analysis by investigating the trends in international investment, averageq, and asset utilization. Because this sample starts in 1981, we evaluate changes in these variables between their average levels over the period 1981 to 1983 compared to their respective average levels in 2019 to 2021. Figure14shows that, except for Canada and the United States, all of the other countries experience a relatively slight decline in averageq. However, similar to the evidence from the United States, we see that investment rates and asset utilization have been declining over the past four decades for all G7 countries.
In TableXII, we estimate the parameters of the traditional and augmentedqmodels for each of the G7 countries. Panel A shows that, except for the United States, the coefficient on averageqis positive and significant in the traditionalqinvestment regression. That is, the negative unconditional relation between investment rates and averageqappears to be unique to the U.S. economy.
Panels B, C, and D of TableXIIreport results from the augmentedqmodel. Similar to the evidence in the United States, including asset utilization as a control variable improves the explanatory power of investment regressions in all G7 countries. Furthermore, there is a significant change in the magnitudes of the coefficients on averageq, indicating that this variable was proxying in part for asset utilization in the traditionalqmodel. Finally, the elasticity of investment to asset utilization is uniformly positive and statistically significant at traditional confidence levels for each of the other G7 countries.
The international evidence raises the question of why the decline in asset utilization is so similar across countries given that financial, labor, and product markets likely vary across these economies. One potential explanation for this commonality is that the cost structure of firms around the globe is becoming increasingly more rigid over time. To test this hypothesis, we replicate the analysis in TableXIusing international data to examine how the cost-sales relation has evolved over time. The results, reported in TableXIII, indicate that similar to the United States, the sensitivity of costs to changes in sales has also been declining for most countries, suggesting that rigidities may be a global phenomenon in developed economies. Italy is the only country that does not experience a decline in the cost-sales sensitivity. This is an interesting result since Italy also experienced the smallest decline in asset utilization among the G7 countries.
Overall, the international evidence confirms our main findings that expected excess capacity is an important determinant of corporate investment policy.
article.8 Conclusion:
When testing Tobin's model of corporate investment, researchers typically use averageqas a proxy for marginalq, as the latter is unobservable. However, this assumption is problematic. Theory states that when managers expect excess capacity in the future, marginalqwill deviate from averageq, as the likelihood of underutilizing new capital reduces the marginal benefit of investing. Under these conditions, averageqwill be a biased proxy for marginalqand thus a key source of measurement error in investment regressions.
This paper addresses this issue by incorporating a proxy for expected excess capacity in investment regressions. This augmentedqmodel performs reasonably well in explaining investment behavior and produces coefficient estimates consistent with economic intuition. Contrary to previous evidence in the literature, we find that the coefficients on averageqin time-series regressions are generally positive, as theory would suggest. Moreover, highlighting the importance of expected excess capacity on investment decisions, we find that asset utilization is an important determinant of investment.
In contrast to previous studies examining corporate investment, our analysis reveals that after accounting for anticipated excess capacity, Tobin's model has worked consistently throughout the 1974 to 2021 period. As such, we find no discernible evidence of a structural break occurring post-1996. These results suggest that Tobin'sqmodel has always worked as originally theorized, but measurement error due to the presence of excess capacity has obscured our ability to observe a positive relation betweenqand investment.
Our main results are robust. The augmented model outperforms the traditionalqmodel in time-series regressions, whether analyzing levels or changes, and in panel data regressions. Furthermore, the model performs well in out-of-sample tests, implying that our results are not driven by spurious in-sample correlations. Consistent with predictions of the expected capacity hypothesis, we find that the augmented model works best for firms that are more likely to experience excess capacity. Robustness tests also indicate that asset utilization is not proxying for financial constraints.
Over the last several decades, the rate of corporate investment has declined significantly. This phenomenon is linked to a robust, secular decline in asset utilization. As excess capacity builds up in the economy, the wedge between average and marginalqincreases, reducing the incentive to invest. This result seems to explain the well-documented and paradoxical negative relation between aggregate investment and averageq. We also find evidence that investment rates and asset utilization are declining in other developed economies.
We explore several explanations for the decline in asset utilization and find that this global phenomenon in the developed world is tied to the growing prevalence of rigidities in prices and wages. During negative economic shocks, these rigidities appear to prevent managers from adjusting product prices and production costs to stimulate demand, leading to a permanent erosion in asset utilization.
Finally, our paper shows that incorporating intangible assets into measures of productive capital when analyzing investment behavior is critical. Failing to do so severely overestimates the productivity of capital, particularly in firms with high levels of intangibles. Researchers should note that the underreporting of intangibles in the NIPA data as published by the BEA is severe.
